sources.title: Can You Judge Art Objectively? — Just Write
sources.content:
YouTube video transcript (with timestamps):

[0:01] So there's a particularly flawed idea about media criticism that has gained a lot of popularity recently.
[0:07] It's the idea that we can judge art
[0:10] quote-unquote objectively. A kind of criticism that focuses exclusively on things like
[0:15] plotholes and whether the events of a story makes strict logical sense.
[0:19] The goal of this criticism is to establish that a piece of art is objectively good or bad.
[0:25] I find this position pretty damaging to film discussions as a whole
[0:29] so today I'm going to explain all the many reasons there is no such thing as
[0:35] objective criticism. It's going to take me quite a while to get there,
[0:39] so I just want you to hold on to this one idea before we begin. The Dark Knight, the movie every film bro keeps on
[0:46] a shrine at his bedside has more plot holes than virtually any other movie
[0:50] you can name. Just hold on to that real tight because we'll come back to it.
[0:55] So first, let's get the semantics straight.
[0:57] You can make objective statements about art. Star Wars was written by George Lucas,
[1:03] there are spaceships in it, the main character is Luke Skywalker. These are called...
[1:11] facts. You enter subjectivity the moment you make a qualitative statement about those facts.
[1:17] George Lucas is a brilliant writer. These spaceships are cool. Luke Skywalker is well-written. These are what's called...
[1:26] opinions. I know this is some simple stuff so far
[1:30] but I need to say it because the idea I'm reacting against is the all too common phrase, "This movie is
[1:37] objectively bad," which presents an opinion as a fact. The proponents of this idea will assert that the
[1:44] objective critic is only speaking about the tangible facts of the matter, while so called subjective critics only talk about their feelings.
[1:51] But this is a false binary.
[1:53] It's totally possible to speak entirely emotionlessly about the themes of a particular work.
[2:04] For instance,
[2:05] I could say that the original Star Wars film is about why nature is superior to
[2:10] technology. And I could point to the climax where Luke puts away the targeting system and
[2:15] embraces the force and that doing so helps him defeat the
[2:18] technologically superior
[2:20] Empire.
[2:20] Nowhere in that analysis have my
[2:23] emotions factored in but I haven't described an objective quality of the film either. I've used evidence from the text to support
[2:30] a position and in the process
[2:32] I've revealed something about my subjective perspective. But someone else could say the scene isn't really about nature versus technology,
[2:40] it's about atheism
[2:41] versus religion. The rebels won because Luke had faith, while the Empire lost because they belittled religion. "Your sad devotion
[2:48] to that ancient religion has not helped you conjure up the stolen data tapes.
[2:53] Or given you clairvoyance enough to find the rebels hidden for..."
[2:57] We've come to two slightly different readings of the same film and neither of them is
[3:03] objective. They are steeped in the perspectives of whoever believes them to be true and both of our hypothetical
[3:09] critics here could use their analysis as a
[3:12] reason to claim that the movie is well-made and they would be speaking just as
[3:16] subjectively as someone claiming it is well-made because the plot makes logical sense.
[3:20] They are opinions based on evidence that are held up to a personal standard.
[3:26] But this lands us in a bit of a pickle that is a sticking point for a lot of people. If all value judgments are
[3:32] subjective, then how do we know what's true? Doesn't that make everyone's opinion equally valid?
[3:37] I mean, why can't we just agree on a standard of quality and then judge everything against that? Wouldn't that be objective?
[3:43] Well, these are questions that plagued two 18th century thinkers most responsible for how we think about this today David Hume and Immanuel Kant.
[3:53] Historically speaking, thinking that you can judge art objectively is not entirely unheard of. To many ancient and medieval
[4:00] thinkers, the idea of beauty being located in an object was a very popular position.
[4:06] It was as objectively true to say that a rose is red as to say that it is beautiful.
[4:12] But in 1757, Desmond, I mean David Hume published "Of the Standard of Taste" which
[4:17] relocated beauty to the eye of the beholder.  "Beauty is no quality in things themselves.
[4:23] It exists merely in the mind which contemplates them; and each mind perceives a different beauty. One person may perceive
[4:30] deformity where another is sensible of beauty and every individual ought to acquiesce in his own sentiment without pretending to regulate those of others."
[4:38] Oh, you sweet summer child!
[4:39] If you had only lived to see YouTube! For Hume, you need a perceiver to exist for beauty to exist.
[4:46] But of course this creates the problem we're running into. If
[4:49] everything is subjective, then how can we make any truth claim about a piece of art? Hume's answer is in the title – taste.
[4:56] Basically, people can improve their taste over time and make well-founded judgements that we can trust.
[5:01] "Strong sense, united to delicate sentiment, improved by practice,
[5:05] perfected by comparison, and cleared of all prejudice, can alone entitle critics to this valuable
[5:10] character; and the joint verdict of such wherever they are to (be) found, is the true standard of taste and beauty."
[5:16] So Hume's answer is we can't know what's good for sure
[5:21] but we can get pretty close by listening to an expert and that anyone can become an expert with practice.
[5:27] It's also better if we listen to critics as a whole instead of just one person
[5:30] because by joint verdict he means that critics tend to come to a
[5:34] consensus on great art over time. Basically, since great pieces of writing tend to withstand the test of time,
[5:41] the fact that they have been well regarded by so many critics in so many times and places, functions like an objective standard. Making it
[5:49] uncontroversial to say that Hamlet is an excellent play for instance. It's sort of like polling data. A few decades later
[5:56] Immanuel Kant was a little disappointed by Hume's essay, thinking that he hadn't gone far enough to solve our problem. In 1790,
[6:02] he published The Critique of Judgment, a
[6:04] landmark and enormously influential book that tried to get as close as possible to figuring out how we
[6:10] perceive the world around us. And I mean how we perceive the
[6:14] entire world around us. Kant isn't just talking about art here and is actually trying to find a way to describe
[6:20] why art is different from just about every other kind of object we encounter. So, I'm not gonna read all this out because Kant doesn't
[6:28] write like a regular human, but instead like a computer describing math equations.
[6:32] But what he says here is that taste is inherently subjective.
[6:35] When we feel pleasure or pain when looking at something it has nothing to do with that object.
[6:40] It is taking place within our own minds, the essence of subjectivity. Now as Kant often
[6:46] does, he splits things into categories. When you experience pain or pleasure, the object
[6:51] responsible for that belongs in one of three groups, actually four,
[6:55] but we can safely ignore the sublime for the purposes of this discussion, since it alone would be its own hour-long video.
[7:01] Ok, so three categories.
[7:03] There's the agreeable, the beautiful and the good. The main difference between them is based on which we desire.
[7:09] Let's say you're starving and a man offers you food.
[7:12] Because you need the food, you're in no position to judge how it tastes. You can only say that it is agreeable.
[7:18] Likewise, our judgments of what is good are also prejudiced by our desires. We want things that fulfill their function
[7:25] so we say that they are good if they do.
[7:27] But when we look at something like a flower, we can call it beautiful because we don't need it for a specific purpose.
[7:33] It's an unbiased judgment.
[7:35] There's another important distinction between the agreeable and the beautiful and it helps us solve part of the problem of total subjectivity.
[7:42] Things that are tied up in the idiosyncrasies of our senses can never be anything more than simply agreeable.
[7:48] You may find the color purple agreeable,
[7:50] I prefer blue and there is no conversation that we can really have there. Our senses just like different stuff.
[7:56] But the beautiful is different. When we see something beautiful,
[7:59] we actually feel that we have a good reason to believe that everyone should see it the same way as us.
[8:04] We actually believe it to be a true
[8:07] quality of the object, even though it isn't. This phenomenon is what Kant calls and bear with me through all of these terms:
[8:14] Subjective
[8:15] Universality. Yeah, that is that is a big weird phrase. So, I know what you're thinking. This sounds like an oxymoron.
[8:22] How can something be universal if it is
[8:25] subjective? And I hear you.
[8:27] But Kant is using this phrase to talk about the fact that while we can't
[8:31] irrefutably prove that we are right about something being beautiful,
[8:34] what matters here is the fact that we feel like we have a good reason to believe that other people should agree with us.
[8:40] We have a
[8:41] justifiable opinion. It's in this space that all conversations around art take place.
[8:47] So to recap Kant's theory so far: taste is inherently subjective.
[8:51] However, to talk about the beauty of something you can't be biased towards it.
[8:55] You cannot need it or see practical value in it and the terminology of the agreeable
[9:01] let's us set aside totally abstract personal opinions from those we think should have universal validity.
[9:07] Whoo boy!
[9:07] That is a lot of stuff! Kant is literally the most difficult literary critic to figure out. But there is
[9:14] one more distinction to clear up and it's between the beautiful and the good and I think this one helps to explain the impossibility of
[9:21] objective critique. I said earlier that things that are good have a
[9:25] specific purpose and we can say objectively whether or not they are good at achieving that purpose.
[9:30] The weird thing about things that are beautiful, is that while they don't serve practical purposes,
[9:35] they feel like they do. So, when we say that a flower is beautiful, we're not talking about the practical purpose
[9:41] it serves as the reproductive organ of a plant. What's beautiful about a flower is that it feels like it was designed to please,
[9:48] Even though that is not its purpose.
[9:50] Man, Kant really does talk about flowers
[9:51] a lot! A piece of art is a little different than nature though
[9:54] since we are constantly debating what the purpose of a piece of art is. A painting or a film doesn't serve an obvious
[10:02] practical function. And even if the author
[10:04] creates it for a specific purpose, art tends to take on a life of its own and can fulfill many unforeseen
[10:10] functions and this is really important. If there's one idea I want you to take away from this video
[10:14] it's this one: because how can you establish the objective criteria for a piece of art when art doesn't serve a definitive
[10:22] describable purpose? Like you can say that a particular
[10:25] hammer is a good hammer because it is good at
[10:29] hammering nails since hammering nails is the purpose of a hammer.
[10:32] But with art, the perceiver has to invent the purpose of whatever they're looking at.
[10:38] Is it to entertain, provoke thought, communicate a message,
[10:41] stir emotions or nothing at all? With every piece of media you encounter, those questions are up for debate.
[10:48] So if 

[…truncated…]
